title: Setting the twist: skeining and finishing handspun yarn
subtitle: Soak, snap, and dry to stabilise a handspun skein
excerpt: Freshly spun yarn holds active twist that will cause knitting or weaving to bias unless it is set. Winding into a skein, soaking in warm water, and drying under light tension permanently relaxes the twist angle and produces balanced, stable yarn.
sourceNotes: Twist-setting by wet finishing is standard practice described consistently in handspinning instruction. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: A loose coil of yarn wound to a consistent circumference. Skeining allows a yarn to be soaked, snapped, and dried in an open form rather than compressed on a bobbin.
A hand tool with a central shaft and two offset crossbars used to wind yarn into a skein of consistent circumference after spinning. The figure-eight winding path creates the skein.
Stabilising the twist angle in a spun yarn by wetting, snapping, and drying under light tension. Set yarn does not kink or bias in use.
A plied yarn in which the twist in the singles and the plying twist cancel each other out. A balanced yarn hangs in a loop without curling or twisting back on itself.

content.text: A freshly spun yarn on a bobbin has active, unset twist that will cause fabric to bias or unravel unevenly.
relaxes the twist angle permanently. Winding into a
first opens the yarn so water can penetrate evenly. A
after finishing hangs in a loop without rotating.
Step 1: Wind the skein on the niddy-noddy

in your non-dominant hand with the shaft vertical. Hook the yarn end over one arm and wind in a figure-eight path: up and around the top arm, down and around the bottom arm, back up to the top, and so on. Wind to the full length of the yarn, then tie the tail to the starting end. Tie the skein in 4 places with loose figure-eight ties in a contrasting yarn or string to prevent tangles during washing.

Fill a bowl or sink with warm water (not hot). Submerge the skein and press it gently to remove air. Leave it to soak for 20 minutes. Do not agitate the skein: agitation during soaking can cause felting.

Lift the skein from the water and squeeze out excess water without wringing. Hold one end of the skein in each hand and snap it firmly 3-4 times to even the twist through the full length. You should feel a slight bounce as the twist redistributes. Snap again if the skein still feels springy or lively.

Hang the skein over a dowel or smooth rod. Hang a small weight from the bottom of the skein (a clothes peg with a bag of coins, or a wooden spoon) to keep it open under light tension while it dries. Do not use heavy weights, which can stretch the yarn permanently. Allow to dry completely before winding into a ball.

items.symptom: Set skein still kinks when wound into a ball
items.cause: Not enough twist was removed: the yarn was not soaked long enough, or the snap was insufficient.
items.fix: Re-wet the skein and snap more firmly. If it still kinks, the original singles were over-twisted. There is no complete fix for that in finishing, but using the yarn in a structure that controls bias (such as balanced two-ply) reduces the visible effect.

items.cause: Ties were too loose or the skein was agitated in the water.
items.fix: Always use 4 ties, tied firmly enough that the loops cannot slip. Lower the skein into still water and leave it: do not stir or squeeze while soaking.
